SUMMARY:
To make the collected biological data understandable and improve the efficacy of these biosensors, scientists have integrated machine learning (ML) with biosensors to analyze large sensing data through various ML algorithms. Different ML models provide benefits in terms of data processing and analysis, including feature extraction, classification, dimension reduction, image recognition, and prediction; when using ML models in biosensors, it is essential to choose a suitable model according to the signal features and application. Recent advancements in machine learning enabled portable and wearable biosensors
================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Sachin Kadian a 1, Pratima Kumari b 1, Shubhangi Shukla a, Roger Narayan a

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talo.2023.100267](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talo.2023.100267 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2666831923000875&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •The manuscript describes recent developments in machine learning (ML)-enabled noninvasive [biosensors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/bioinstrumentation "Learn more about biosensors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). 
*   •The manuscript describes the basic features of ML algorithms used in [data processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/data-transmission "Learn more about data processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). 
*   •The manuscript describes the application of different ML models in biosensors for healthcare applications. 
*   •The manuscript considers the challenges associated with ML-enabled biosensors, including data privacy. 

Abstract
--------

Recent advances in noninvasive portable and wearable biosensors have attracted significant attention due to their capability to offer continual physiological information for continuous healthcare monitoring through the collection of biological signals. To make the collected biological data understandable and improve the efficacy of these biosensors, scientists have integrated machine learning (ML) with biosensors to analyze large sensing data through various ML algorithms. In this article, we have highlighted the recent developments in ML-enabled noninvasive biosensors. Initially, we introduced and discussed the basic features of ML algorithms used in data processing to build an intelligent biosensor system and the capability to make clinical decisions. Next, the principles of portable and wearable biosensors, the application of different ML models in diverse biosensors for healthcare applications, and their impact on the performance of biosensors are discussed. The last section highlights the challenges (such as data privacy, consistency, stability, accuracy, scalable production, and adaptive learning capacity), future prospects, and necessary steps required to address these issues, spotlighting their revolutionizing impact on the healthcare industry for the development of next-generation ML-enabled efficient biosensors.

Keywords
--------

Machine learning

Clinical decision making

Portable and wearable biosensors

Introduction
------------

Over the past decade, the emerging demand for [biosensors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/bioinstrumentation "Learn more about biosensors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in different sectors, including personalized health monitoring, clinical practice, drug screening, food safety, and environment safety, has attracted significant attention from the scientific community. Biosensors often provide simpler detection approaches, high specificity, low cost, versatility, and portability [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0001), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0002), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0003), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0004), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0005), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0006), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0007). Biosensors are the subset of sensors for use in measuring biological or chemical changes through a sensing element that is integrated with a physicochemical transducer; these devices are mainly used for diagnostic applications [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0008), [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0009), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0010), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0011). The history of biosensors times back to 1958 when Clark and Lyons invented the first true biosensor for oxygen detection; since then, various advances have been reported involving the use of nanotechnology and effective signal amplification strategies to widen the diversity of analytes, enhance the sensing efficacy, as well as enable the biosensors to be portable, user-friendly, noninvasive, and wearable [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0009),[[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0012), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0013), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0014)].

The word "nanotechnology" refers to small-sized materials with at least one dimension less than ∼100 nanometers, which involves the use of four types of [nanomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), including zero-dimensional, one-dimensional, two-dimensional, and three-dimensional materials [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0015), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0016), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0017), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0018), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0019), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0020). Due to their exceptional biological, electrical, chemical, and physical properties, [nanomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanocrystalline-material "Learn more about nanomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been found to play a substantial role in advancing the sensing performance of biosensors [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0014),[[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0021), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0022), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0023), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0024), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0025)]. However, all point-of-care devices suffer from issues such as poor sensitivity, signal noise, accuracy, stability, and [selectivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/selectivity "Learn more about selectivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which limit their translation to commercial use [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0026), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0027), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0028), [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0029), [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0030). Therefore, to address these issues, the scientific community is moving towards using sensor arrays instead of single sensors to enhance the overall performance of biosensors [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0031). For instance, recent developments in [conducting polymers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/conducting-polymer "Learn more about conducting polymers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and metal oxide-based electronic noses utilize more than five sensors with partial specificity. Although sensor-array-based devices are garnering significant attention for various biomedical applications, the translation of high-dimension data collected from the sensor array into understandable and useful information serves as a barrier to commercialization [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0031), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0032), [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0033). Hence, to resolve this problem, the research community developed another breakthrough by incorporating ML with biosensors to analyze high-dimension sensing data through various ML algorithms. In this direction, several advanced ML algorithms such as [support vector machine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/support-vector-machine "Learn more about support vector machine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (SVM), Naive Bayes (NB), random forest (RF), decision tree (DT), κ-nearest neighbor (κNN), feedforward [artificial neural network](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/artificial-neural-network "Learn more about artificial neural network from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (feedforward ANN), gradient-boosted trees (GBT), convolutional [neural network](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/neural-network "Learn more about neural network from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CNN), and [recurrent neural network](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/recurrent-neural-network "Learn more about recurrent neural network from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (RNN) have emerged in recent years and are used as an effective tool for [data processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/data-transmission "Learn more about data processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and analysis in materials science [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0034). Similarly, a few of these approaches have also been applied to sensors in order to overcome the data-related challenges and provide them with intelligence by automatic prediction of the concentration of analyte through ML algorithm-based decision system. For instance, neural networks have been employed in physical sensors such as acceleration and strain sensors for objection detection, gesture, and activity recognition [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0035), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0036), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0037). Additionally, researchers and scientists have also explored more user-friendly ML technologies, including AutoML, Federated learnings, and explainableAI, to increase the confidence of the healthcare industry as well as make ML an integral part of clinical applications for diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0038). However, some advanced ML algorithms have not been considered by the biosensor community. This situation is possibly due to the complexity of collecting large-quantity data by biosensing systems, which is required to train the ML models. Nowadays, with significant development in wearable and portable biosensors, data collection has become more straightforward, which feeds the expansion of ML-enabled biosensors. Different ML models provide benefits in terms of data processing and analysis, including feature extraction, classification, dimension reduction, image recognition, and prediction; when using ML models in biosensors, it is essential to choose a suitable model according to the signal features and application. The selection of an appropriate ML model plays a vital role in the transformation of unintelligible raw data into valuable and logical information, which leads to the development of efficient and intelligent biosensors for use in disease diagnosis, food safety, drug screening, and personalized health monitoring.

This review highlights the recent advancements in ML-enabled portable biosensors and how advanced ML models enhance the performance of biosensors. Initially, we consider the basic features of ML, such as data cleaning, data transformation, feature extraction, and hyperparameter tuning, followed by various ML algorithms that have been used to build an intelligent sensing system along with the capability to make clinical decisions. Next, the principles of portable and wearable biosensors as well as their compatibility with the different ML models, are highlighted. Further, the application of different ML models in diverse biosensors for healthcare applications and their impact on the performance of biosensors are discussed. Finally, the challenges and future prospects in the field of ML-enabled biosensors are highlighted.

### Machine learning

The introduction of digitization has improved clinical medicine by expediting the analysis of clinical results, reducing the possibility of human error, improving the handling of patient information, and monitoring health data over time [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0039),[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0040)]. Machine learning, a subfield of artificial intelligence, plays a key role in improving healthcare [[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0041),[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0042)]. The general process of machine learning follows standard steps in order to develop a machine learning model for a problem (as shown in [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#fig0001)). The first and most crucial step in machine learning is data pre-processing, which helps extract the essential information from the raw data and eliminates noise and outliers from the data [[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0043),[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0044)]. Data pre-processing consists of four major steps, which entail data cleaning, data integration, data transformation, as well as data reduction steps [[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0045),[46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0046)]. Further, the pre-processed data is divided into train, validate, and test sets for the training, parameter tuning, and testing of the model. In the next step, the machine learning model is trained on a training set with a model weight initialization method [[47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0047),[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0048)]. The validation dataset is subsequently used to tune the hyperparameters; finally, the performance of the machine learning model is evaluated on the testing set by utilizing different evaluation metrics [[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0049),[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0050)]. The machine learning algorithm is selected from a pool of varieties to develop the model based on the problem that needs to be solved. The following sections explain each step and provide information on different machine learning models.

![Image 11: Fig 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666831923000875-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (411KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666831923000875-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (411KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666831923000875-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Step by step flowchart of developing a machine learning model.

Pre-processing of collected biosensor data is one of the crucial tasks in machine learning for extracting knowledge. Collected data may have missing values, noise, and outliers; these issues are handled by the process of data cleaning[[51]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0051). If some data is missing, it is treated by ignoring the tuple or filling the missing value by mean and median. In order to eliminate the noise from the data, regression, the binning method, or clustering can be used [[52]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0052). The values that lie beyond the clusters are considered as outliers and can be removed from the dataset. Another important step of data pre-processing is data integration, in which the data is collected from multiple sources and combined into a single data set [[53](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0053),[54](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0054)]. Further, with the process of data transformation, data is converted from one format into the desired format [[55]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0055). For instance, different types of data normalization methods are applied to bring the values of different attributes in a similar range [[56]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0056). Normalization plays an essential role in machine learning; if data is not normalized, the attributes with a higher value range mostly influence the model and its outcome [[57]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0057). Standardization and min-max normalization are the most popular normalization techniques. Finally, data reduction is one of the crucial steps in data pre-processing, which helps in reducing the volume of the dataset while preserving the underlying important information [[58]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0058). Data reduction is required in cases where the data is very large and redundant, which leads to enhanced data handling and avoids model overfitting. Dimensionality reduction, feature selection, feature extraction, sampling, clustering, and compression are different types of feature reduction [[59]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0059). For instance, dimensionality reduction involves reducing the number of attributes while retaining as much information as possible and overcomes the problem of the curse of dimensionality [[60](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0060),[61](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0061)]. Feature selection refers to the process of selecting a subset of input attributes from the dataset, which are most relevant to the output variable. Similarly, in feature extraction, multiple original attributes are combined to form new attributes so that new attributes capture the essence of the original dataset while keeping the dimension of the dataset as low as possible [[62](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0062),[63](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0063)]. Some of the most used feature extraction techniques are linear [discriminant analysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/discriminant-analysis "Learn more about discriminant analysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LDA), principal component analysis (PCA), t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE) as well as uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP) [[64](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0064),[65](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0065)].

Once the raw dataset is pre-processed, it needs to be split into three subsets: the training set, the validation set, and the test set [[66]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0066). The training set is used to train the machine learning model in order to learn the patterns in the dataset. The validation set is used to understand the performance of the machine learning model and fine-tune the hyperparameters of the model. Finally, the test set is used to evaluate the performance of the final ML model. Generally, the dataset is divided with a size of approximately 60% for training, 20% for validating, and 20% for testing the model [[67](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0067),[68](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0068)].

Model weight initialization is a significant task in the training phase of the ML model. The initial values of the weights play a key role; if initialized correctly, it can help in achieving the optimized values of the model quickly; otherwise, converging to a minimum may become challenging [[69]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0069). Random initialization is the commonly used initialization method. However, if the randomly initialized value is very high or low, it may suffer from the problem of slow convergence, vanishing gradients, or exploding gradients. Xavier initialization and He initialization use an extra scaling factor to overcome the drawbacks of random initialization [[70]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0070).

A machine learning model is a mathematical model in which several parameters must be learned from the data [[71]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0071). These hyperparameters of an ML model are tuned in the validation phase in order to determine the optimal values that enhance the accuracy of the model. There can be several parameters of a model, including the number of hidden layers, batch size, learning rate, number of neurons in each layer, number of trees in RF, and depth of trees. To find the optimal values for these hyperparameters, different approaches such as grid search, manual search, random search, bayes optimization, [bayesian optimization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/bayesian-optimization "Learn more about bayesian optimization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and genetic algorithms are used. The performance status of the model is reported by the loss curve. It can help compare the impact of different hyperparameters and decide on the best values. Also, loss curves can help in understanding the scenarios of underfitting and overfitting. Underfitting takes place when the model is too simple, such that it does not learn the patterns in the training data accurately and performs poorly on both training and testing data. Overfitting happens when a model is too complex and starts learning the noise in the training data. It performs very well on training data but poorly on unseen testing data [[72]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831923000875#bib0072).

### Machine learning algorithms

In recent years, with advances in machine learning and data collection, several machine learning algorithms have been applied in biosensors to process and model biosensor data. These algorithms include [support vector machine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/support-vector-machine "Learn more about support vector machine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (SVM), principal component analysis (PCA), decision trees (DTs), random forests (RF), k-means clustering, k nearest neighbor (kNN), [feedforward neural network](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/feedforward-neural-network "Learn more about feedforward neural network from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (FFNN), recurrent neural network (RNN), and [convolutional neural network](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

SUMMARY:
The integration of wearable sensor technology and machine learning algorithms has significantly transformed the field of intelligent medical rehabilitation.

--- SOURCE 3: A Predictive Framework for Real-Time Health Monitoring via ... ---

SUMMARY:
We identify many challenges facing machine learning applications on wearable devices from design to deployment, such as different deployment alternatives, storage, power consumption, user acceptance, reliability, communication, security, and privacy. Additionally, we review the different privacy-preserving techniques used for machine learning training and inference and discuss their applicability to the model of wearable device usage shown in Figure 1. Thus, the main focus of this study was to identify the challenges of developing machine learning applications for healthcare wearable devices and alternative solutions found in the literature. In Section 3, different challenges facing machine learning research for wearable devices are reviewed, relevant privacy and security aspects for machine learning applications in IoMT are discussed, and possible solutions in literature are presented.

Machine Learning for Healthcare Wearable Devices: The Big Picture
=================================================================

[Farida Sabry](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Sabry%20F%22%5BAuthor%5D)

PMCID: PMC9038375 PMID: [35480146](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35480146/)

Abstract
--------

Using artificial intelligence and machine learning techniques in healthcare applications has been actively researched over the last few years. It holds promising opportunities as it is used to track human activities and vital signs using wearable devices and assist in diseases' diagnosis, and it can play a great role in elderly care and patient's health monitoring and diagnostics. With the great technological advances in medical sensors and miniaturization of electronic chips in the recent five years, more applications are being researched and developed for wearable devices. Despite the remarkable growth of using smart watches and other wearable devices, a few of these massive research efforts for machine learning applications have found their way to market. In this study, a review of the different areas of the recent machine learning research for healthcare wearable devices is presented. Different challenges facing machine learning applications on wearable devices are discussed. Potential solutions from the literature are presented, and areas open for improvement and further research are highlighted.

1. Introduction
---------------

The last few years have witnessed great advances in wearable technologies. Wearable devices include any device that can be worn by humans such as wristwatches, glasses, chest straps, rings, and prosthetic sockets. Wearable devices belong to the Internet of medical things (IoMT), together with the implantable, ambient, and stationary devices used in hospitals. These devices are typically connected to a network and communicate remotely with mobile devices as shown in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#fig1).

### Figure 1.

![Image 9: Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/51b0/9038375/fc54f50c16e9/JHE2022-4653923.001.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/figure/fig1/)

Wearable device application model.

Wearable devices may include different types of sensors to continuously monitor various human signals, e.g., temperature sensors, accelerometers, optical sensors, and biometric sensors. Although the readings of some of these sensors are not yet as accurate as stationary devices in hospitals, they are sometimes considered acceptable [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B1), [2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B2)], depending on the application.

Sensors in IoMT devices and human interaction with these devices are considered a big source of data from which features can be extracted for machine learning (ML) algorithms to detect and learn useful patterns. This can be very useful in many healthcare and elderly care applications such as activity detection for health state assessment, fall detection, stress detection, fitness tracking, vital signs monitoring, and diseases' diagnosis. Using machine learning techniques to learn from human body signals, recorded by wearable devices, has been an active research area in the last decade with a lot of published research studies. Despite this huge research effort and the remarkable growth in using wearable devices, especially smart watches, few machine learning applications for wearable devices have found their way into the market.

Examples include irregular rhythm notification feature [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B3)] in Apple Watch, which won U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval with a long list of warnings and precautions in 2018 ([https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/cdrh_docs/reviews/DEN180044.pdf](https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/cdrh_docs/reviews/DEN180044.pdf)), and Eko's heart murmur detection algorithm, which has been recently published [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B4)], which is not really for a personal wearable device but an electronic stethoscope. Additionally, some of the wearable devices that were used for monitoring, which were surveyed in [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B5)], are no longer available in the market. Practical and reliable use of machine learning techniques in the domain of wearable devices is still facing many challenges.

Several review papers have discussed some challenges for wearable devices. In a survey paper published in 2012 [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B6)], the authors focused on some features of wearable devices and their types such as diseases that can be monitored, research prototypes, and challenges such as system efficiency, user perception, cost, social inclusion, and ethical issues. In [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B5)], the authors provided a survey of commercially available wearable devices at that time (2017). They focused on communication security issues, power efficiency, and wearable computing. Neither of the surveys focused on the challenges facing machine learning applications for healthcare wearable devices specifically.

In this study, we review recent applied machine learning research for wearable devices. We identify many challenges facing machine learning applications on wearable devices from design to deployment, such as different deployment alternatives, storage, power consumption, user acceptance, reliability, communication, security, and privacy. We discuss security and privacy both from the data and the model perspectives listing potential solutions to keep subjects' personal data from wearable devices private and secure. Additionally, we review the different privacy-preserving techniques used for machine learning training and inference and discuss their applicability to the model of wearable device usage shown in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#fig1).

The review includes the recent research papers in the field of wearable devices that have been published from 2017 to December 2021 to answer the following questions:

1.   What are the healthcare machine learning tasks that have been researched in the literature, the body signals, and techniques used in these tasks?

2.   What are the challenges facing machine learning for healthcare wearable devices?

3.   What are the possible solutions for these challenges in literature?

Thus, the main focus of this study was to identify the challenges of developing machine learning applications for healthcare wearable devices and alternative solutions found in the literature. Different categories of recent healthcare machine learning research are reviewed while spotting the challenges and highlighting potential research areas and applications that need further investigation.

The rest of the review is organized as follows. In the next section, the necessary background for IoMT and the different human body signals used in wearable devices research are presented. Moreover, applications for machine learning in IoMT are reviewed and categorized referencing some of the recent research work published in each area. In [Section 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#sec3), different challenges facing machine learning research for wearable devices are reviewed, relevant privacy and security aspects for machine learning applications in IoMT are discussed, and possible solutions in literature are presented. In [Section 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#sec4), we discuss these solutions, their applicability, and their shortcomings. Additionally, we highlight the main research gaps we perceived in the domain. Finally, the study conclusions are provided in [Section 5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#sec5).

2. Wearable Devices and Machine Learning
----------------------------------------

The wearable device domain is being actively researched for the sake of enhancing ease of use, comfort, and noninvasiveness of monitoring physiological vital signs and sometimes psychological or emotional state, which can be detected by analyzing data from different sensors. Following the tremendous technological advances in the design of system on chip (SoC), the development and use of wearable devices have remarkably achieved high growth rates in the last few years. The wearable device market size was valued at USD 32.63 billion in 2019 and is expected to expand at high rates in the next few years according to the wearable technology market industry report by Grand View Research ([https://www.grandviewresearch.com/industry-analysis/wearable-technology-market](https://www.grandviewresearch.com/industry-analysis/wearable-technology-market)).

The number of globally connected wearable devices is about to reach 1 billion according to Statista ([https://www.statista.com/statistics/487291/global-connected-wearable-devices/](https://www.statista.com/statistics/487291/global-connected-wearable-devices/)). Examples of wearable devices are smart watches, armbands, chest straps, shoes, helmets, glasses, lenses, rings, patches, textiles, and hearing aids [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B6)].

Despite this grand growth, there is still a great need for ongoing research in this area for enhancing the accuracy of these devices, using different body signals for new application areas, and dealing effectively with the complexity of the human body.

### 2.1. Wearable Device's Signals Used in Learning

The human body can be seen in an abstract way as a group of systems (circulatory system, nervous system, respiratory system, digestive system, etc.). It receives a group of inputs and releases a set of outputs as shown in [Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#fig2). Inputs include the inhaled air, water, food, visual input for all the scenes and objects seen during the day, auditory input for all sounds and voices heard, sensory inputs for the things touched, and olfactory input for things smelled during the day. Outputs include the exhaled air, excretions such as urine, feces, and sweat, skin moisture, body temperature, blood in case of injuries and laboratory tests, energy released by the human in terms of body movements, or performing mental activities and voice output, which can be normal speech, singing, or shouting. Analysis of the inputs and outputs can, to some extent, predict the health state of a person, diagnose possible diseases/disorders, and assist with therapeutic suggestions. These inputs and outputs need to be monitored by wearable devices worn during the day.

#### Figure 2.

![Image 10: Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/51b0/9038375/d5e0edd3f877/JHE2022-4653923.002.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/figure/fig2/)

Human body as a system and signals that can be used as a source of data for machine learning models.

Wearable devices include any device mounted on the body and can capture noninvasive signals from the human body through the use of different types of sensors. There are numerous well-known signals and signs that are read from the human body in literature to identify the vital signs and other information about the health or mental state of the subject. Examples of these sensors include skin temperature sensor used in [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B7), [8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B8)] and electrodermal activity (EDA) sensor or sometimes known as galvanic skin response (GSR) sensor used on the skin to record the skin conductance that varies with the sympathetic state of the subject [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B2)]. Other examples include an electrocardiogram (ECG) sensor to capture electrical changes in the skin corresponding to heartbeats used in [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B9)–[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B12)]. To capture features of the electrical activity of the brain and the health of muscles and the nerve cells, electroencephalogram (EEG) and electromyography (EMG) sensors are used [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B13)–[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B19)]. Blood volume pulse (BVP) can be captured using an optical photoplethysmography (PPG) sensor to estimate heart rate and heart rate variation as in [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B1), [20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B20)–[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B22)]. PPG sensor [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B23)] is also used to give an approximation for the oxygen saturation in blood (_SpO_ 2) as in [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B22), [24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B24)]. Accelerometers, gyroscopes, and magnetometers are often used in a wide variety of applications to capture or recognize body movement and activities, which can tell a lot about the health and the lifestyle of the person [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B1), [25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B25)–[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B39)].

Other signals that have been used sparingly in literature include electrogastrogram (EGG), which records the electrical activity of the stomach [[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B40)], and electrooculogram (EOG), which is generated by eye movements and can be measured with electrodes placed around the eye [[41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B41)]. Sensory and especially olfactory inputs are challenging to model, but it was observed that the human body has different autonomic responses to different odors, which can be analyzed through the GSR and ECG signals [[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B42)]. These inputs can be used in applications including personalized treatments based on odors and foods for neuropsychiatric and eating disorders. Some other inputs may need visual monitoring. [Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#fig3) summarizes the different sensors used in the literature for different machine learning healthcare tasks. Features are extracted from these signals to learn a model for either classification or regression of a certain variable. Some literature studies use statistical values such as mean, minimum, maximum, mode, variance, standard deviation, entropy, and kurtosis. However, it is often hard to interpret how some of these statistical features affect the classification or the outcome variable. Additionally, model's accuracy is usually negatively affected by adding more irrelevant features as more is not always better, and domain-specific features that are expressive achieve better performance [[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B43)]. Estimating heart rate and breathing rate as features from the PPG signal, change in acceleration magnitude, jerk of motion, and transient changes in skin resistance for seizure detection are examples of domain-specific features. Some applications are concerned with changes happening over a long time period, and some are concerned with transient changes due to certain events such as fall detection and emotion recognition.

#### Figure 3.

![Image 11: Figure 3](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/51b0/9038375/8ae534fbd52c/JHE2022-4653923.003.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/figure/fig3/)

Healthcare machine learning tasks and sensors used for each one in literature.

### 2.2. Machine Learning for Wearable Devices

Machine learning involves getting wearable devices to act/take decisions without explicit programming for a specific scenario through learning from past experiences. As it is well known, machine learning is usually classified as either supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, or reinforced according to the type of the available training data. Learning from past experiences is encoded in terms of data examples that can be either labeled or unlabeled. The target variable for labeled data can be categorical or numerical. Among the tasks that involve machine learning are classification in case of the categorical target output variable, regression in case of numerical labels, and clustering for unlabeled data. Most machine learning research for wearable devices belongs to the classification tasks, some are for clustering [[44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B44)–[46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B46)], and few can be tackled as regression problems [[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B43)].

Applied research to explore applying machine learning techniques using the body signals discussed in the last subsection for health monitoring, elderly care, and fitness tracking has been growing over the last decade. Among the areas that got researchers' attention are fall detection, seizure detection, vital sign monitoring and prediction [[47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#B47)], and activity recognition for fitness tracking or identifying human daily activities. Additionally, wearable devices have been researched for their use in stress detection and detection of heart rate arrhythmia and rehabilitation tasks. Tables [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#tab1)–[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9038375/#tab3) show the different areas and a sample of the most recent research work done in each area. The table also shows the machine learning technique(s), sensor(s), and dataset(s) used in each study.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Machine learning algorithms enhance data analysis of biosensors by processing large datasets, extracting meaningful patterns, and transforming raw signals into interpretable health metrics.
Employing predictive models, wearable biosensors leverage historical data to forecast health trends and anomalies, facilitating timely medical interventions and improving patient outcomes.
Machine learning enables personalized health insights by analyzing individual data, tailoring health recommendations, and adapting monitoring strategies to enhance patient engagement and adherence.

Integrating machine learning with wearable biosensors allows for real-time health monitoring, providing instantaneous feedback on physiological changes and enabling immediate responses to health issues.
